stem: Apply the Ratio Test to \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{[[m]][[c]]^{n}}{n!} \): compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \left|\dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}\right| \).
solution: The constant \( [[m]] \) cancels in the ratio: \( \dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_{n}} = \dfrac{[[m]][[c]]^{n+1}/(n+1)!}{[[m]][[c]]^{n}/n!} = \dfrac{[[c]]}{n+1} \), so \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{[[c]]}{n+1} = [[answer]] \); since \( L=0 < 1 \), the series converges absolutely for every \( [[c]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the factorial growth as if it did not affect the ratio at all, keeping only the exponential's base.
This wrongly assumes the ratio test is inconclusive (\( L=1 \)) rather than computing the actual limit.
This subtracts one from the base of the exponential instead of dividing by the growing factorial ratio.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.

stem: Apply the Ratio Test to \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{n^{[[p]]}}{[[c]]^{n}} \): compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \left|\dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}\right| \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the correct ratio, reporting the exponential base itself instead of its reciprocal.
This wrongly divides the polynomial degree by the exponential base rather than taking the limit of the ratio of consecutive terms.
This wrongly assumes the polynomial-degree factor forces the whole ratio to vanish.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.

stem: Apply the Ratio Test to \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{[[m]]\,(2n)!}{(n!)^{2}\,[[c]]^{n}} \): compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \left|\dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}\right| \).
solution: The constant \( [[m]] \) cancels in the ratio: \( \dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_{n}} = \dfrac{(2n+2)!/((n+1)!)^{2}[[c]]^{n+1}}{(2n)!/(n!)^{2}[[c]]^{n}} = \dfrac{(2n+2)(2n+1)}{(n+1)^{2}\,[[c]]} \), and as \( n\to\infty \) the polynomial ratio \( (2n+2)(2n+1)/(n+1)^{2}\to 4 \), so \( L = \dfrac{4}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the double-factorial ratio contributes a factor of \( 1 \) instead of \( 4 \).
This inverts the correct ratio, multiplying by \( [[c]] \) instead of dividing by it.
This uses a factor of \( 2 \) instead of the correct limiting factor of \( 4 \) from the binomial-type ratio.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.

stem: Apply the Root Test DIRECTLY (without first writing \( a_{n} \) as an \( n \)th power) to \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{[[a]]n+[[b]]}{[[c]]^{n}} \): compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_{n}|} \).
solution: Write \( \sqrt[n]{|a_{n}|} = \dfrac{([[a]]n+[[b]])^{1/n}}{[[c]]} \). Since any polynomial in \( n \) raised to the power \( 1/n \) tends to \( 1 \) as \( n\to\infty \) (its \( n \)th root grows sub-exponentially), \( L = \dfrac{1}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the correct value, reporting the exponential base itself instead of its reciprocal.
This wrongly keeps the linear coefficient \( [[a]] \) as if it survived the \( n \)th root, rather than tending to \( 1 \).
This wrongly assumes the polynomial numerator forces the whole \( n \)th root to vanish.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.
